title: XmlPath for XML Responses
overview: Parse and validate XML API responses using XmlPath.
whyItMatters: While JSON is dominant, many legacy enterprise systems and SOAP web services still communicate using XML. Testing these endpoints requires XML traversal skills.
explanation: REST Assured supports XML validation out of the box using `XmlPath` (which internally leverages GPath for XML). You can assert XML node values directly in the `body()` block. To access attributes (rather than text nodes), you prefix the attribute name with an `@` symbol. You can also extract the response and parse it manually using `XmlPath` just like `JsonPath`.
keyPoints: Navigate XML nodes using dot notation.
Support for XML namespaces is available but requires configuration.
Works well for SOAP responses and hybrid REST/XML endpoints.
realWorldExample: When testing a legacy SOAP payment gateway, the response comes back as an XML envelope. You use `XmlPath` to navigate down to the `<TransactionStatus>` node and assert its text is 'APPROVED'.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the `@` symbol when trying to read an XML attribute.
Struggling with XML namespaces. If the XML uses namespaces (e.g., `soapenv:Body`), you may need to configure `XmlPath` to be namespace-aware or use wildcard syntax.
Applying JSON logic (like array indexes) incorrectly to XML node lists.
bestPractices: If dealing with complex SOAP XML, consider extracting the payload and using dedicated XML libraries (like XPath in Java) if GPath becomes too cumbersome.
Ensure you request XML using `.accept(ContentType.XML)` if the API supports multiple formats.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you validate an XML attribute (e.g., id="1") in REST Assured?
interviewQuestions.a: You use the `@` symbol before the attribute name in your path, like `body("root.element.@id", equalTo("1"))`.

interviewQuestions.q: Does REST Assured support SOAP web services?
interviewQuestions.a: Yes, REST Assured can test any HTTP endpoint. You can send a SOAP envelope as a String in the POST body, set Content-Type to text/xml, and validate the XML response using XmlPath.

revisionSummary: Use XmlPath to assert XML nodes (dot notation) and attributes (@).
